title: What is a variable
instruction:
Imagine a labeled shelf slot in a library.

You write "title" on the slot label. You put "The Hobbit" on the shelf. Whenever you need that book, you just say the label — and Go hands you what's on that shelf.

That is a variable. A labeled slot that holds a value.

Run this program and see it in action.
successMessage: You stored a book title and retrieved it. That is what every variable in every library system ever written does.

title: Create your own variable with :=
instruction:
`:=` is how you create a variable and give it a value at the same time.

Left side: the shelf label. Right side: the book that goes on it.

Change the title to your favorite book and run it.
hint: Replace "The Hobbit" with your favorite book title. Keep the quotes.

codeChecks.message: Change the value from "The Hobbit" to your own favorite book title.

successMessage: Your first original variable. := creates it and fills it in one shot, like shelving a new book.

title: Declare first, assign later
instruction:
Sometimes you want to create the shelf slot first and place the book later.

`var title string` creates an empty slot labeled title.
`title = "The Hobbit"` puts a book on it.

Notice: filling later uses `=` not `:=`

Run this.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var title string
	title = "The Hobbit"
	fmt.Println(title)
}
successMessage: Two separate steps: create the shelf slot, then put the book on it. This comes in handy when the book hasn't arrived yet.

title: The var keyword with a value
instruction:
`var` can also declare and assign in one line — just like `:=` but more explicit.

`var copies int = 5`

This says: create a variable called copies, it holds whole numbers, and it starts at 5.

Change the copy count to how many copies your library has and run it.
hint: Change the number 5 to your actual copy count. Keep everything else the same.

codeChecks.message: Keep the var keyword and int type. Just change the number.

successMessage: var is the longer form. := is the shortcut. Both work — use whichever feels clearer.

title: Update a variable
instruction:
Variables can change. A book count might go up as new books arrive.

But there is one rule:
- `:=` creates a new variable. Use it once.
- `=` updates an existing one. Use it after.

The count starts at 60. A new shipment arrives — update it to 95 and print both values.
hint: After fmt.Println(copies), write: copies = 95  then fmt.Println(copies) again.

codeChecks.message: Update copies using a single =  like this: copies = 95
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Same shelf slot, new count. = refills. := creates. Never mix them up.

title: Two variables in one line
instruction:
Go lets you declare and assign multiple variables at once.

`title, author := "The Hobbit", "Tolkien"`

Left side: the labels. Right side: the values. Order matches.

Change the book and author to your own and run it.
hint: Replace "The Hobbit" with your book title and "Tolkien" with its author.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	title, author := "The Hobbit", "Tolkien"
	fmt.Println(title)
	fmt.Println(author)
}

codeChecks.message: Keep the multi-assignment format: title, author := ...
Change the book title and author to your own.

successMessage: One line, two variables. Go matches left to right: title gets the first value, author gets the second.

title: Declare a group with var ()
instruction:
When you have several library details, you can declare them as a group.

```
var (
    title  string = "The Hobbit"
    copies int    = 25
    genre  string = "Fantasy"
)
```

Clean, organized, easy to read.

Add a fourth variable called `section` with your library section (like "Fiction" or "Reference"), then print all four.
hint: Add section string = "YourSection" inside the var block. Then add fmt.Println(section) at the bottom.

codeChecks.message: Add section inside the var block: section string = "YourSection"
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: The var block keeps all your library catalog fields together. Much easier to scan than a pile of := lines.

title: Zero values
instruction:
What happens if you create a shelf slot but never put a book in it?

Go does not leave it empty. It gives it a default value called a zero value.

- `string` gets `""`  (empty title)
- `int` gets `0`
- `bool` gets `false`

Run this and see the zero values for your library data.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var title string
	var copies int
	var available bool
	fmt.Println(title)
	fmt.Println(copies)
	fmt.Println(available)
}
successMessage: No surprises in Go. Every variable always has a value, even before you catalog a single book.

title: Fix the broken names
instruction:
Variable names have rules:

- Start with a letter, not a number
- No spaces — use camelCase instead
- Case matters: `copies` and `Copies` are different variables

This code has three broken names. Fix them so the program runs.
hint: 1st name starts with a number. 2nd name has a space. 3rd is the wrong case when printed.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	1stBook := "Dune"
	library name := "Central Library"
	AvailableCopies := 15
	fmt.Println(1stBook)
	fmt.Println(library name)
	fmt.Println(availableCopies)
}

codeChecks.message: Fix the invalid variable names. No numbers at the start, no spaces — use camelCase.

successMessage: Good eye. Valid names keep your library code readable and error-free.

title: Build a library profile from scratch
instruction:
Everything you just learned — now use it all.

Build a short library profile using these rules:
- Use `:=` for title and copies
- Use `var` with a type for section
- Declare available with var and assign it separately
- Print all four

No starter code. Just you.
hint: title := "...", copies := 10, var section string then section = "...", var available bool then available = true. Then four Println calls.

codeChecks.message: Declare section using the var keyword
Declare available using the var keyword

successMessage: You used every form of variable declaration in Go. Next up: the different kinds of data variables can hold.
